feedback.text: I support this initiative because the 2024 fitness check showed that current EU rules still leave big gaps in protecting consumers online. People continue to face manipulative design, hidden costs, and unfair targeting, causing significant harm across the EU. Dark patterns are one of the clearest problems. Many websites and apps use tricks like hiding the reject all button on cookie banners, fake urgency timers, or preselected addons. These practices push people into choices they wouldnt normally make. They should be clearly banned. Addictive design also deserves attention. Features like endless scroll, autoplay, loot boxes, and streak rewards are deliberately built to keep people engaged and spending. This is especially harmful to children and vulnerable users, and rules are needed to limit such designs. Another related concern is the "Stop Killing Games" campaign, which highlights how online-only games can be made unplayable when publishers shut down servers or revoke access. This deprives buyers of content they paid for, erodes digital ownership, and prevents cultural preservation. The campaigns EU petition has gathered over 1.4 million signatures and may lead to legislation requiring developers to include offline modes or communityhostable versions when retiring online games. I consider this directly tied to unfair digital practices and consumer rights. Influencer marketing is another weak spot. Too often, followers dont realize when content is sponsored. Both influencers and the brands behind them should be held responsible for clear disclosure, so consumers can make informed choices. Unfair personalisation is also a serious risk. For example, offering different prices or pushing inapp purchases based on a persons past behavior or emotional state can exploit them. People should know when personalisation is being used, and limits should prevent unfair or manipulative targeting. The rules should also make basic rights easier to use. For instance, cancelling a subscription should always be as simple as signing up. This would close one of the most common consumer complaints. Finally, it is important that these new rules work well alongside the GDPR, the DSA, and other EU laws, while giving regulators stronger tools for crossborder enforcement. Harmonisation will help consumers and make compliance clearer for honest businesses. Overall, I support the Digital Fairness initiative and encourage the EU to move forward quickly to close these gaps. Without clear rules and effective enforcement, manipulative design, unfair targeting, and the disappearance of paid digital content will continue to undermine consumer trust, ownership, and well-being.
